message:
chore(beads): close extension discovery epic (bd-2uh1) + children

All 6 children closed: registry protocol, search CLI, info CLI,
curated collections, quality signals, tests.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -370,13 +370,13 @@
 {"id":"bd-2tx","title":"No-mock policy enforcement (lint + CI guard)","description":"Goal:
- Enforce the no-mock policy by adding a lightweight CI guard that flags new mock/fake usage in tests, with actionable logging.

Scope:
- Define explicit allowlist rules (file + rationale) for any exceptional mocks.
- Add a fast check (rg/ast-grep) that fails CI when Mock/Fake/Stub patterns appear outside allowlist.
- Emit clear diagnostics (file:line + matched symbol) and guidance to replace with VCR/real deps.
- Document the policy and how to request/justify exceptions.

Acceptance Criteria:
- CI fails if new mocks/fakes are introduced without allowlist entry.
- Output includes exact file:line matches and remediation guidance.
- Allowlist is minimal, audited, and documented.
- Guard is fast, deterministic, and runs in CI + locally.

Dependencies:
- Leverages bd-351 audit to seed allowlist and baseline mock inventory.","acceptance_criteria":"[ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss
[ ] Unit tests cover core success/failure + edge cases for this bead (add regression coverage if applicable)
[ ] Integration/E2E script exercises the end-to-end path (or explicitly marks N/A with rationale in notes) and emits detailed JSONL logs + artifacts per bd-4u9
[ ] Logs include inputs, outputs, timing, and redaction summary; artifacts list is deterministic
[ ] Any new fixtures/golden outputs are deterministic and documented
[ ] Quality gates pass: cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test
[ ] Docs/fixtures updated if behavior or UX changes","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T04:59:27.589230634Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T19:25:34.207195880Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T09:43:09.429120256Z","close_reason":"Completed: CI no-mock code guard + remove MockProvider","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2tx","depends_on_id":"bd-26s","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T00:00:02Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-2tx","depends_on_id":"bd-351","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-04T00:00:02Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2u2s","title":"Static scan: identify valid extension entry points in corpus","description":"# Static scan: identify valid extension entry points in corpus

## Context
We have 1,167+ TS files in tests/ext_conformance/artifacts/. Not all are extension entry points. Some are utilities, configs, or sub-modules. We need to identify which files are actual extension entry points.

## What Makes a Valid Entry Point
From pi-mono loader.ts (legacy_pi_mono_code/pi-mono/packages/coding-agent/src/core/extensions/loader.ts):
1. File must export a default function (the factory function)
2. The factory receives ExtensionAPI parameter
3. Common pattern: export default function(pi: ExtensionAPI) { ... }
4. Alternative: export default function init(api) { ... } (dynamic typing)
5. Multi-file extensions have index.ts or package.json with pi.extensions field

## What To Do
1. For each TS file, scan for: export default function, export default async function, export default (arrow function)
2. Check for ExtensionAPI type reference (strong signal) or pi.on/pi.registerTool/pi.events (weak signal)
3. Check directory structure: index.ts in a dir = likely entry point
4. Check package.json files for pi.extensions field
5. Output: JSON array of {path, confidence: high|medium|low, patterns_found: [...]}

## Key Patterns (from CompatibilityScanner in src/extensions.rs lines 127-414)
The Rust CompatibilityScanner already does similar work. We should match its patterns:
- ExtensionAPI import
- export default function
- registerTool calls
- pi.on / pi.events calls
- pi.session calls
- UI primitives (header, footer, overlay, widget, spinner)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every TS file in artifacts/ is scanned
- Output JSON classifies each as: entry_point, sub_module, non_extension, or unknown
- False positive rate < 5% (verified by spot-checking)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:18:10.397065595Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T07:36:15.024720761Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T07:36:15.024639710Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2u2s","depends_on_id":"bd-2dnl","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:18:10.449483570Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":316,"issue_id":"bd-2u2s","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Scanner complete: 1193 files → 305 entry points, 765 sub-modules, 117 non-extensions, 6 unknowns (0.5%). Tests: 3 passing. Output: docs/extension-entry-scan.json. Test file: tests/ext_entry_scan.rs. Patterns detected: export_default_function, ExtensionAPI_import, registerTool, registerCommand, registerProvider, event_hook, etc. Handles ExtensionFactory pattern, test files, .d.ts exclusion.","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:35:13Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2u8g","title":"Conformance: Standalone popular community extensions (10 exts)","description":"Conformance tests for standalone popular community extensions from various authors. Extensions (10): 1. pi-cost-dashboard (mrexodia) — Dashboard for API cost monitoring 2. pi-canvas (jyaunches) — Interactive TUI canvases (calendar, document, flights) 3. pi-notification-extension (lsj5031) — Telegram/bell alerts when agent finishes 4. pi-ssh-remote (cv) — Redirects all file operations to remote host via SSH 5. pi-dcp (zenobi-us) — Dynamic context pruning for intelligent optimization 6. pi-notify (ferologics) — Native desktop notifications via OSC 777 7. pi-ghostty-theme-sync (ogulcancelik) — Sync Ghostty terminal theme with pi session 8. pi-sketch (ogulcancelik) — Quick sketch pad, draw in browser, send to models 9. pi-screenshots-picker (Graffioh) — Screenshot picker for better selections 10. pi-super-curl (Graffioh) — Empowers curl with coding agent capabilities. Each from a different author, testing maximum diversity of extension patterns.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-05T06:19:15.370077254Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T01:38:10.987519180Z","closed_at":"2026-02-06T01:38:10.987377807Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2u8g","depends_on_id":"bd-1oi7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-05T06:19:15.383078367Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
-{"id":"bd-2uh1","title":"Epic: Extension Discovery and Search — user-facing browsing, search, and extension info","description":"# Goal
Build the user-facing discovery layer so users can find, evaluate, and install extensions without needing to know exact package names or GitHub URLs ahead of time.

# Background
The package manager (src/package_manager.rs) already handles install/remove/update from npm, git, and local sources. The extension catalog (bd-3kp3) covers the internal manifest for pinning and corpus tracking. But there is NO user-facing discovery mechanism:
- Users cannot search for extensions by keyword
- Users cannot browse categories
- Users cannot see extension details before installing
- There is no quality signal (popularity, compat score, maintenance status)

This is the difference between "apt install package-name" (exists) and "apt search keyword" + "apt show package-name" (doesnt).

# Why This Matters
- Extension ecosystems live or die by discoverability — VSCode Marketplace, npm search, crates.io
- Without discovery, adoption is bottlenecked by word-of-mouth and documentation
- The conformance corpus already has 60+ validated extensions — users should be able to find them
- This is table stakes for any extension platform

# Scope
1. Registry protocol: how extension metadata is published and queried
2. CLI commands: pi search, pi info, pi list --available
3. Rich terminal rendering: extension READMEs, install counts, compat scores
4. Curated collections: categories and featured lists
5. Quality signals: conformance tier, last-updated, Pi version compatibility

# Out of Scope
- Hosting a central registry server (use GitHub API + npm registry as backends for now)
- Extension submission/review process (manual curation initially)
- Monetization or paid extensions

# Architecture
- Data source: GitHub API for repo metadata + npm registry for package metadata
- Local index: JSON cache of discovered extensions, refreshed periodically
- Search: Client-side fuzzy search over cached index (no server needed)
- Rendering: rich_rust tables for search results, glamour markdown for README rendering
- Categories: tags in extension manifest (pi.json or package.json pi field)

# Dependencies
- Depends on bd-3kp3 (Extension catalog, pinning, corpus manifest) for manifest schema
- Relates to bd-ofcj (Expand proven Pi extension coverage) for corpus to search over
- Relates to bd-34io (Classify & score candidates) for quality signals

# Children
1. Extension registry protocol spec + index schema
2. pi search CLI command
3. pi info CLI command (rich detail view)
4. Curated collections and category taxonomy
5. Quality signals computation and display","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:35:20.108245219Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T07:58:09.018450686Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cli","extensions","ux"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1","depends_on_id":"bd-34io","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:01:25.343192162Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1","depends_on_id":"bd-3kp3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:01:24.967841239Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":502,"issue_id":"bd-2uh1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"ARCHITECTURAL CONTEXT: The package manager (src/package_manager.rs) handles install/remove/update. This epic builds the DISCOVERY layer on top. Think of it as: package_manager = pip install, this epic = PyPI search + package detail pages.

DESIGN DECISION — NO CENTRAL SERVER: We deliberately avoid hosting a registry server. Instead, we query GitHub API and npm registry as data sources, and cache the results locally. This keeps the system decentralized and avoids the operational burden of running infrastructure. The curated manifest is a static JSON file in the Pi repo.

SEARCH IMPLEMENTATION: Client-side fuzzy search over the cached index. This is fast enough for 1000+ extensions and avoids server round-trips. If the corpus grows beyond 10K, we can add SQLite full-text search (the dependency already exists via asupersync).

QUALITY SIGNALS ARE CRITICAL: The difference between "show me extensions" and "show me GOOD extensions" is the quality signal layer. Without it, users install broken/abandoned extensions and have a bad experience. The conformance tier (from our test suite) is the most valuable signal because it's the only one we control.

DEPENDENCY: bd-3kp3 (Extension catalog, pinning, corpus manifest) must define the manifest schema first. This epic consumes that schema. bd-34io (Classify & score candidates) provides the quality signals we display.

ESTIMATED EFFORT: Registry protocol (6-8h), pi search (8-10h), pi info (6-8h), collections (4-6h), quality signals (6-8h). Total: ~30-40 engineering hours.","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:02:41Z"},{"id":515,"issue_id":"bd-2uh1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (EPIC-LEVEL):
- [ ] All 6 subtasks (registry, search, info, collections, quality, tests) closed
- [ ] pi search <query> returns relevant results from cached index
- [ ] pi info <name> shows extension details with rendered README
- [ ] Quality signals visible in search results (conformance tier, maintenance status)
- [ ] Offline mode: search works on cached index when no network
- [ ] Tests: 34+ cases, all pass, structured JSONL logs emitted
- [ ] Quality gates: cargo fmt, cargo check, cargo clippy, cargo test all pass","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:58:09Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2uh1.1","title":"Extension registry protocol spec and local index schema","description":"# Goal
Design the extension registry protocol — how extension metadata is fetched from remote sources (GitHub, npm), stored locally, and queried for search/browse operations.

# Background
The package manager (src/package_manager.rs) already knows how to install from npm and GitHub. But there is no structured index of AVAILABLE extensions. Users must know exact package names. This task designs the metadata layer that enables discovery.

# Deliverables

1. Remote data sources:
   - GitHub: Search GitHub repos with topic "pi-extension" via GitHub API
   - npm: Search npm registry for packages with "pi-extension" keyword
   - Curated list: static JSON manifest in a Pi-maintained repo (for quality-vetted extensions)

2. Local index schema (SQLite or JSON):
   {
     "version": 1,
     "last_refreshed": "2026-01-15T10:30:00Z",
     "extensions": [
       {
         "id": "auto-commit-on-exit",
         "name": "Auto Commit on Exit",
         "description": "Automatically commits changes when Pi session ends",
         "version": "1.2.0",
         "source": { "type": "npm", "package": "@user/pi-auto-commit" },
         "author": "username",
         "license": "MIT",
         "tags": ["git", "automation", "lifecycle"],
         "conformance_tier": 1,
         "pi_version_range": ">=0.5.0",
         "downloads_30d": 1500,
         "last_updated": "2026-01-10T00:00:00Z",
         "readme_url": "https://...",
         "repository_url": "https://..."
       }
     ]
   }

3. Refresh strategy:
   - On first "pi search": fetch and cache index
   - Auto-refresh if cache older than 24 hours
   - Manual refresh: "pi update-index"
   - Incremental: only fetch new/updated since last refresh

4. Search algorithm:
   - Fuzzy text match on name + description + tags
   - Weighted scoring: name match (3x) > tag match (2x) > description match (1x)
   - Sort by relevance * quality_signal (conformance tier, downloads)

# Files to Create/Modify
- New: src/extension_index.rs (index management, search, refresh)
- src/package_manager.rs: Wire index into install flow
- src/config.rs: Add index cache path

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Schema documented in docs/extension-registry.md
- [ ] Index refresh from GitHub + npm sources works
- [ ] Fuzzy search returns relevant results
- [ ] Cache invalidation after 24h
- [ ] Index stored in ~/.pi/agent/extension-index.json","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonMill","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:37.363576504Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T19:28:22.543081221Z","closed_at":"2026-02-06T19:28:22.543058038Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["discovery","extensions","protocol"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.1","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:37.363576504Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":520,"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"DESIGN REFINEMENT (from review):

The registry spec MUST be offline-first:
1. Ship a bundled seed index with the Pi binary (embedded at compile time)
2. First search uses seed index if no network (no "please wait" on first run)
3. Background refresh when network becomes available
4. Stale cache warning ("Index is 7 days old, run pi update-index to refresh")
5. Never fail on network error — always fall back to cached/seed index

This matters because:
- New users running pi search for the first time shouldnt wait for a network fetch
- Users on restricted networks (corp firewalls, planes) should still discover extensions
- The seed index can be updated with each Pi release (60+ known extensions)","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:58:41Z"},{"id":560,"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Progress update (CrimsonMill): wired extension-index runtime integration and shorthand source resolution.

Code changes:
- src/lib.rs: export `extension_index` module
- src/config.rs: add `Config::extension_index_path()` + `PI_EXTENSION_INDEX_PATH` override
- src/package_manager.rs:
  - add `PackageManager::resolve_install_source_alias()`
  - resolve non-explicit/non-local shorthand sources via local index in `parse_source()`
  - preserve local-path precedence when path exists
  - add test `parse_source_prefers_existing_local_paths_over_index_aliases`
- src/main.rs: package install/remove/update now canonicalize shorthand aliases before package-manager ops + persist canonical source
- docs/extension-registry.md: document env override and current runtime wiring

Validation:
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/pi-agent-rust-target-crimsonmill cargo check --all-targets` ✅
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/pi-agent-rust-target-crimsonmill cargo clippy --lib --bin pi -- -D warnings` ✅
- focused tests ✅:
  - `cargo test --lib extension_index::tests::`
  - `cargo test --lib parse_source_prefers_existing_local_paths_over_index_aliases`
  - `cargo test --lib directory_helpers_`
- `cargo fmt --check` and full `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` are currently blocked by pre-existing unrelated workspace findings (not introduced by this bead).
","created_at":"2026-02-06T19:10:51Z"},{"id":564,"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Second progress update (CrimsonMill): completed remote refresh + command wiring.

Additional implementation since prior note:
- `src/extension_index.rs`
  - best-effort remote refresh APIs (`refresh_best_effort`, `load_or_refresh_best_effort`)
  - npm search adapter + parser mapping to index entries
  - GitHub search adapter + parser mapping to index entries
  - merge/upsert logic preserving existing metadata when incoming fields are missing
  - explicit request timeout constant for refresh HTTP calls
  - unit tests for npm parse, GitHub parse, and merge behavior
- `src/cli.rs`: `update-index` subcommand (`Commands::UpdateIndex`) + parse test
- `src/main.rs`: subcommand wiring + handler; `pi config` now displays extension index path
- `tests/conformance/fixture_runner.rs`: command serialization now handles `UpdateIndex`
- `docs/extension-registry.md`: refresh semantics + runtime wiring updated

Validation (current state):
- `cargo fmt --check` ✅
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/pi-agent-rust-target-crimsonmill cargo check --lib --bins` ✅
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/pi-agent-rust-target-crimsonmill cargo clippy --lib --bin pi -- -D warnings` ✅
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/pi-agent-rust-target-crimsonmill cargo check --test conformance_fixtures` ✅
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/pi-agent-rust-target-crimsonmill cargo clippy --test conformance_fixtures -- -D warnings` ✅
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/pi-agent-rust-target-crimsonmill cargo check --all-targets` ❌ blocked by pre-existing unrelated `src/compaction.rs` test breakage
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/pi-agent-rust-target-crimsonmill cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` ❌ blocked by pre-existing unrelated `src/compaction.rs` + `src/extensions_js.rs` findings
","created_at":"2026-02-06T19:26:10Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2uh1.2","title":"pi search CLI command — search available extensions by keyword","description":"# Goal
Implement the "pi search <query>" CLI command that searches the extension index and displays results in a rich terminal table.

# Background
This is the primary user-facing discovery command. It queries the local extension index (built by the registry protocol spec task) and presents results in a scannable format.

# Usage
  pi search git          # Search for git-related extensions
  pi search "auto commit" # Phrase search
  pi search --tag provider # Filter by tag
  pi search --sort downloads # Sort by popularity
  pi search --limit 20     # Limit results

# Output Format (rich_rust table)
  Name              Version  Description                        Downloads  Tier
  auto-commit       1.2.0    Auto commit on session exit         1,500     T1
  git-branch-hook   0.5.0    Switch branches via /git command      890     T2
  git-stats         2.0.1    Show git statistics in session        650     T1

  3 extensions found. Install with: pi install <name>

# Implementation Plan
1. Add "search" subcommand to clap CLI (src/cli.rs)
2. In main.rs, route to search handler
3. Search handler:
   a. Load or refresh extension index
   b. Apply fuzzy search with query
   c. Apply filters (--tag, --tier, --source)
   d. Sort results (--sort: relevance (default), downloads, name, updated)
   e. Render rich_rust table with columns: Name, Version, Description, Downloads, Tier
   f. Print install hint at bottom

4. Auto-refresh:
   - If index doesnt exist or is >24h old, refresh before searching
   - Show "Refreshing extension index..." message during refresh
   - Cache refreshed index for subsequent searches

# Files to Modify
- src/cli.rs: Add search subcommand with args
- src/main.rs: Route search subcommand
- New: src/extension_search.rs or add to extension_index.rs

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] "pi search git" returns relevant results
- [ ] Results sorted by relevance by default
- [ ] --tag filter works
- [ ] --sort flag works (relevance, downloads, name, updated)
- [ ] Rich table output with colors
- [ ] Install hint shown at bottom
- [ ] Auto-refresh of stale index

pi search should work BOTH as a CLI command AND as an interactive TUI slash command:
1. CLI: pi search "git" (already planned)
2. Interactive: /search git (within an active session)

For the interactive version:
- /search opens an inline search results view in the conversation viewport
- Results are clickable (Enter to install)
- /info <name> shows detail view inline

-{"id":"bd-2uh1.3","title":"pi info CLI command — rich extension detail view with README rendering","description":"# Goal
Implement "pi info <extension>" CLI command that shows detailed information about an extension including rendered README, capabilities, compatibility status, and install instructions.

# Usage
  pi info auto-commit-on-exit

# Output Format
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ auto-commit-on-exit v1.2.0               │
  │ by username • MIT License                │
  │ Conformance: Tier 1 (fully tested)       │
  │ Pi compatibility: >=0.5.0                │
  │ Downloads (30d): 1,500                   │
  │ Last updated: 2026-01-10                 │
  │ Tags: git, automation, lifecycle         │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ README                                    │
  │                                           │
  │ Automatically commits your changes when   │
  │ a Pi session ends. Configurable via...    │
  │                                           │
  │ ## Configuration                          │
  │ ...                                       │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Capabilities Required                     │
  │ • exec: git commands                     │
  │ • tool/read: .git/config                 │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Install: pi install npm:@user/pi-auto... │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘

# Implementation Plan
1. Add "info" subcommand to clap CLI
2. Info handler:
   a. Look up extension in index by name/id
   b. Fetch README from source (GitHub API or npm registry)
   c. Parse extension manifest for capabilities
   d. Render rich_rust panel with sections

3. README rendering:
   - Fetch raw markdown from GitHub (raw.githubusercontent.com) or npm readme field
   - Render using glamour (terminal markdown renderer)
   - Truncate to terminal height with "Press q to exit, scroll with arrows" if long
   - Cache fetched READMEs locally (1 hour TTL)

4. Capability extraction:
   - Parse extension source or manifest for hostcall usage
   - Display human-readable capability list

# Files to Modify
- src/cli.rs: Add info subcommand
- src/main.rs: Route info subcommand
- Extension index module: Add detail fetch methods

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] "pi info <name>" shows formatted extension details
- [ ] README rendered as terminal markdown
- [ ] Capabilities listed if available
- [ ] Install command shown at bottom
- [ ] Graceful handling of missing README or unavailable extension
- [ ] Cached README to avoid repeated fetches","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:02.776254423Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T07:07:04.007318479Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cli","discovery","extensions"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:02.776254423Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:01:23.954769933Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2uh1.4","title":"Curated extension collections and category taxonomy","description":"# Goal
Define the category taxonomy for extensions and create curated collections (featured, popular by category, starter packs) that help users discover relevant extensions.

# Background
Raw search only works when users know what to look for. Categories and collections enable browsing — "show me all provider extensions" or "what are the most popular automation extensions?" This is how every extension marketplace (VSCode, Chrome Web Store, Obsidian) drives discovery beyond search.

# Deliverables

1. Category taxonomy:
   - providers: Custom LLM providers (OpenAI, local models, etc.)
   - tools: Additional tools beyond the built-in 7
   - automation: Lifecycle hooks, auto-commit, scheduled tasks
   - git: Git-related extensions (branch management, commit helpers)
   - formatting: Output formatting, rendering, themes
   - data: Data processing, analysis, transformation
   - testing: Test runners, coverage reporters
   - security: Audit, scanning, policy enforcement

2. Curated collections:
   - "Getting Started": 5 essential extensions for new users
   - "Power User": 10 extensions for advanced workflows
   - "Provider Pack": All custom provider extensions
   - "DevOps Toolkit": CI/CD, deployment, monitoring extensions

3. Manifest format for collections:
   {
     "id": "getting-started",
     "name": "Getting Started",
     "description": "Essential extensions for new Pi users",
     "extensions": ["auto-commit-on-exit", "git-branch-hook", ...]
   }

4. CLI commands:
   - pi search --category providers   # Browse by category
   - pi collections                    # List available collections
   - pi collections getting-started   # Show collection details

# Files to Modify
- Extension index module: Add category and collection support
- src/cli.rs: Add collections subcommand
- New: data/collections.json (curated collection definitions)

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Category taxonomy covers all known extension types in corpus
- [ ] At least 3 curated collections defined
- [ ] pi search --category works
- [ ] pi collections command shows available collections
- [ ] Categories derived from extension manifest tags","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:17.011431603Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T06:47:16.109286971Z","closed_at":"2026-02-07T06:47:15.946199840Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["curation","discovery","extensions"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:17.011431603Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:01:24.337971802Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":715,"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Closed. Created docs/extension-collections.json (pi.ext.collections.v1): 10-category taxonomy, extension-to-category mapping for 80+ extensions, 5 curated collections (Getting Started, Power User, Provider Pack, Safety Net, UI Customization). Each collection entry includes reason for recommendation.","created_at":"2026-02-07T06:47:16Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2uh1.5","title":"Quality signals computation and display for extension discovery","description":"# Goal
Compute and display quality signals for extensions in search results and info views, helping users assess extension reliability before installing.

# Background
Not all extensions are equal. Some are well-tested (conformance tier 1), actively maintained, and widely used. Others are experimental, abandoned, or incompatible. Quality signals help users make informed decisions — like star ratings on app stores or crate download counts on crates.io.

# Quality Signals

1. Conformance Tier (from Pi conformance test suite):
   - T1: Fully tested, all assertions pass
   - T2: Mostly tested, minor issues
   - T3: Partially tested, some features fail
   - Untested: No conformance data

2. Maintenance Status:
   - Active: Updated within 90 days
   - Maintained: Updated within 1 year
   - Stale: Not updated in 1+ year
   - Abandoned: Not updated in 2+ years, no response to issues

3. Compatibility Score (from compat scanner):
   - Green: 100% compatible, no Node.js dependencies
   - Yellow: 90%+ compatible, minor shim gaps
   - Red: <90% compatible, significant issues

4. Popularity Metrics:
   - npm downloads (30d) if from npm
   - GitHub stars if from GitHub
   - Install count from Pi telemetry (future)

5. Composite Quality Score (0-100):
   - 40% conformance tier
   - 25% maintenance status
   - 20% compatibility score
   - 15% popularity metrics

# Display Format
In search results table: colored badge (green/yellow/red) + numeric score
In info view: detailed breakdown of each signal

# Implementation Plan
1. Compute signals at index refresh time
2. Store in extension index entry
3. Sort by composite score in search results (default)
4. Display badge in search table column
5. Detailed breakdown in pi info output

# Files to Modify
- Extension index module: Add quality computation
- Search/info display code: Add quality columns/sections

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 5 signal types computed for indexed extensions
- [ ] Composite score calculated with documented weights
- [ ] Quality badge visible in search results
- [ ] Detailed breakdown in pi info
- [ ] Score updates on index refresh","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:28.098522967Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T06:51:38.984615150Z","closed_at":"2026-02-07T06:51:38.826114918Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["discovery","extensions","quality"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.5","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:28.098522967Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.5","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:01:24.650506725Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":717,"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.5","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Done. Added QualitySignals schema (conformance_grade/compatibility_score/maintenance_status/category_tags) and populated for all 223 extensions. Grade distribution: 187 A, 22 B, 9 C, 4 F, 1 untested. Compat: 162 green, 47 yellow, 14 red. 75 extensions have category tags.","created_at":"2026-02-07T06:51:38Z"}]}
-{"id":"bd-2uh1.6","title":"Tests: Extension Discovery E2E suite — search, info, index refresh, offline mode","description":"# Goal
End-to-end test suite for the extension discovery system, validating search, info display, index management, and edge cases like offline mode and stale caches.

# Test Categories

## 1. Index Management Tests (8+ tests)
- First-run: index created from scratch (mock GitHub + npm responses via VCR)
- Index refresh: stale index (>24h) triggers auto-refresh
- Incremental update: only new/modified extensions fetched
- Cache corruption: graceful recovery from malformed index file
- Offline mode: search works on cached index when network unavailable
- Manual refresh: pi update-index forces full refresh
- Index versioning: migration from v1 to v2 schema
- Concurrent access: two pi instances dont corrupt index

## 2. Search Tests (10+ tests)
- Exact name match: pi search "auto-commit" → returns exact match first
- Fuzzy match: pi search "git comit" → returns git commit extensions
- Tag filter: pi search --tag provider → only provider extensions
- Category filter: pi search --category automation
- Sort by downloads: pi search --sort downloads → descending order
- Sort by name: pi search --sort name → alphabetical
- Limit: pi search --limit 5 → exactly 5 results
- Empty results: pi search "xyznonexistent" → helpful message
- Special characters: pi search "c++" → handles special chars
- Long query: pi search "an extension that helps with git management" → reasonable results

## 3. Info Display Tests (6+ tests)
- Basic info: pi info auto-commit → shows metadata panel
- README rendering: README markdown rendered as terminal output
- Missing README: graceful fallback message
- Capabilities section: shows required permissions
- Install hint: correct install command shown
- Not found: pi info nonexistent-extension → helpful error

## 4. Quality Signals Tests (5+ tests)
- Conformance tier displayed correctly (T1/T2/T3)
- Maintenance status computed from last-updated date
- Composite score calculated with correct weights
- Badge colors match risk level (green/yellow/red)
- Score updates on index refresh

## 5. CLI Integration Tests (5+ tests)
- pi search output is valid table (parseable columns)
- pi info output fits terminal width
- pi collections lists available collections
- pi search from within interactive TUI (/search command)
- Error messages include help text

# Logging Requirements
Every test MUST emit structured JSONL logs:
{
  "test": "search_exact_match",
  "timestamp": "2026-...",
  "query": "auto-commit",
  "filters": {},
  "result_count": 3,
  "top_result": { "name": "auto-commit-on-exit", "score": 0.95 },
  "pass": true,
  "duration_ms": 45,
  "index_state": "cached",
  "artifacts": ["search-output.txt"]
}

# Test Infrastructure
- VCR cassettes for GitHub API and npm registry responses
- Mock index files for offline tests
- tempdir for index cache isolation
- Terminal width simulation for output tests

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 34+ test cases covering all 5 categories
- [ ] All tests emit structured JSONL logs
- [ ] Offline mode tested (no network = use cache)
- [ ] VCR cassettes for all network calls
- [ ] All tests pass cargo test","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:05.668559488Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:38.181091267Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["discovery","extensions","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.6","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:05.668559488Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.6","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:37.511051991Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.6","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:37.694974877Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.6","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:37.851665335Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.6","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:38.010487657Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.6","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:38.181042787Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2uhs","title":"Task: Implement Exec Hostcall Handler (pi.exec)","description":"# Task: Implement Exec Hostcall Handler

## Objective

Implement the handler for HostcallKind::Exec that routes pi.exec() calls to shell command execution.

## Background

When an extension calls pi.exec("git", ["status"], {cwd: "/path"}), it should execute the command and return stdout/stderr/exitCode. This is similar to the bash tool but with more granular control.

## TypeScript Reference (loader.ts:215-216)

```typescript
exec(command: string, args: string[], options?: ExecOptions) {
    return execCommand(command, args, options?.cwd ?? cwd, options);
}
```

## Implementation

```rust
impl ExtensionDispatcher {
    /// Handle pi.exec(command, args, options) hostcalls.
    /// 
    /// Executes a shell command with optional working directory and timeout.
    /// Unlike the bash tool, this provides direct command execution without
    /// shell interpretation (no pipes, redirects, etc. unless explicitly using sh -c).
    async fn dispatch_exec(
        &self,
        cmd: &str,
        args: &[String],
        opts: ExecOptions,
    ) -> HostcallOutcome {
        use std::process::Stdio;
        use asupersync::Command;
        
        let cwd = opts.cwd
            .map(PathBuf::from)
            .unwrap_or_else(|| self.cwd.clone());
        
        let timeout = opts.timeout_ms.unwrap_or(120_000); // Default 2 min
        
        // Build command
        let mut command = Command::new(cmd);
        command
            .args(args)
            .current_dir(&cwd)
            .stdin(Stdio::null())
            .stdout(Stdio::piped())
            .stderr(Stdio::piped());
        
        // Set environment if provided
        if let Some(env) = opts.env {
            for (k, v) in env {
                command.env(k, v);
            }
        }
        
        // Execute with timeout
        let result = match tokio::time::timeout(
            Duration::from_millis(timeout),
            command.output(),
        ).await {
            Ok(Ok(output)) => {
                let stdout = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout).to_string();
                let stderr = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr).to_string();
                let exit_code = output.status.code().unwrap_or(-1);
                
                HostcallOutcome::Success(serde_json::json!({
                    "stdout": stdout,
                    "stderr": stderr,
                    "exitCode": exit_code,
                    "signal": output.status.signal(),
                }))
            }
            Ok(Err(e)) => HostcallOutcome::Error {
                code: "EXEC_ERROR".to_string(),
                message: e.to_string(),
            },
            Err(_) => HostcallOutcome::Error {
                code: "TIMEOUT".to_string(),
                message: format!("Command timed out after {}ms", timeout),
            },
        };
        
        result
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default)]
pub struct ExecOptions {
    pub cwd: Option<String>,
    pub timeout_ms: Option<u64>,
    pub env: Option<HashMap<String, String>>,
}
```

## Security Considerations

1. **Path Traversal**: Validate cwd is within allowed directories
2. **Command Injection**: Args are passed directly, not through shell
3. **Resource Limits**: Enforce timeout, consider memory limits
4. **Capability Check**: Requires "exec" capability in extension manifest

## Differences from Bash Tool

| Aspect | pi.exec() | bash tool |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Shell interpretation | None (direct exec) | Full shell |
| Arguments | Array of args | Single command string |
| Pipes/redirects | Not supported | Supported |
| Use case | Precise commands | Complex scripts |

## Testing

1. Unit test: Simple command (echo)
2. Unit test: Command with args
3. Unit test: Custom cwd
4. Unit test: Timeout handling
5. Unit test: Non-zero exit code
6. Integration test: Real command execution

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bd-389q (ExtensionDispatcher struct)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] dispatch_exec() method implemented
- [ ] Command execution with args works
- [ ] Custom cwd respected
- [ ] Timeout handling works
- [ ] stdout/stderr/exitCode returned
